approach: FINDING (#233, folded into master #263 as node op6-m4): operator.feature and pod.feature each carry one omnibus @quality @rubric scenario whose TITLE claims 'in voice' while its rubric carries no voice dimension. A persona scores full marks entirely out of voice. op6-m1 (#243 parse-guard) made both suites parse, so this is an ACTIVE false green, not a silent one.

BRIEF PREMISE CORRECTED: the project README's deferral names 'a voice-rubric dimension across the three persona nodes'. That count is STALE — written when the project had gateway/recruitment/tuning; gateway has since split into pod/+operator/ and tuning was renamed mechanic/. There are FOUR persona nodes today. Only operator and pod carry the title-claims-voice pattern; recruitment and mechanic have real documented voices but their rubric titles claim flow/routing only.

MISS TEST ON THE CURRENT RUBRICS — the defect is wider than the finding states. Both omnibus rubrics are four dimensions summing to max 9 at threshold 7. Zeroing any single dimension: mechanics 0 -> 6 FAIL (decisive); brief 0 -> 7 PASS; routing 0 -> 7 PASS; harness 0 -> 7 PASS. THREE of four dimensions can be totally zeroed and the scenario still passes — only mechanics ever decides. The threshold rebalance is therefore REQUIRED for the edited scenario to clear the op6-m2 discrimination bar, not optional bookkeeping.

DECISION (fork stated to owner, owner chose ADD + all four personas): add a discriminating voice dimension rather than dropping the title claim. Mechanics are fully offloaded to a CLI, so voice is the persona's actual product.

WRONG SUBJECT REVISED BY OWNER: the initial design named the SIBLING-VOICED persona as the discriminating wrong subject (an effusive Operator, a clipped Pod). Owner refuted the premise — personas blend when co-loaded into one context and there is no way to unload one when loading another, so inter-persona distinctness is not a property the runtime produces and a rubric must not lean on it. The named wrong subject is now the DEFAULT-ASSISTANT-VOICED persona: a producer gets every mechanic right and leaves the Output section bland, so the persona renders as helpful-hedging-chatbot. More plausible, and carries no cross-persona dependency. Dimension names contrast against the default, never against a sibling.

SHAPE SPLITS BY NODE, and the split is structural, not arbitrary. operator+pod each have ONE omnibus @rubric grading the whole persona run, whose title already claims voice -> voice folds in as one dimension, max 3, threshold 7 -> 11 (frozen REWRITE, Clearance). recruitment+mechanic have 3 and 7 @rubric scenarios respectively, each grading one flow or routing decision — voice is not a facet of 'does a leash change route to the autonomy rubric', so adding voice to all ten would over-fire. Each gets ONE new dedicated voice @quality @rubric scenario (ADDITIVE, self-clears, no Clearance).

VOICE TUNING (owner: 'nice to have, not a bar'): 'warm' alone is too thin to grade, so Pod = warm and steady (companion to the mission) and Crimp = warm and transactional (sizes you up across a bar). Applied as polish; the rubric does NOT depend on the two being distinguishable from each other. Each voice also carries one concrete TELL, because a tell is visible where an adjective is a vibe, and a vibe is what lets a memorizer score max.

SPEC-SIDE, NOT ONLY SUITE-SIDE: all four node READMEs describe the voice ONLY as intro prose — it is not a ## Use Cases bullet and has no behavior-table row. The suite claimed a bar the spec never specified. Each README gains a voice Use Case bullet + table row so spec and suite agree.
OUT OF SCOPE: ssa-lowering (op6-m5), the case-judge protocol (op6-m3, in flight).

why: Owner granted live in-session after being shown the fork (ADD a voice dimension vs DROP the 'in voice' title claim), the full miss-test arithmetic on the current rubrics, and the finding that three of four existing dimensions are already decorative. Owner chose ADD and extended scope from the two defective nodes to all four personas ('do all'). The grant covers a REWRITE of one @frozen @quality @rubric scenario in each of operator.feature and pod.feature: one voice dimension added (max 3) and the threshold moved 7 -> 11 of 12, which makes every dimension decisive — each single-dimension-zero wrong subject then lands at 9-10, under threshold, and a max-everywhere subject may still drop exactly one point. Scenario titles already claim 'in voice' and become honest rather than changing. recruitment.feature and mechanic.feature are ADDITIVE (one new voice scenario each) and self-clear — this grant is not needed for them and is not claimed over them.

summary: No way to unload a persona when loading another — co-loaded persona instructions blend in one context
why: Raised by the owner while refuting the sibling-swap wrong subject for the voice rubric. Two persona gateway skills loaded into the same context mix their instructions, and nothing unloads the first when the second is invoked, so no persona's rendered voice is reliably attributable at runtime. This does not block the voice rubric — each @rubric scenario evaluates one persona in its own scenario context, which stays well-defined — but it bounds what persona-voice distinctness can mean in practice and is why this CR's rubric contrasts each voice against default-assistant prose rather than against a sibling. Outside this touch-set (cyberfleet/persona-voice); it is a persona-loading architecture concern.

summary: The structural edit-class classifier is blind to @rubric docstring content — a frozen rubric can be gutted post-freeze and the gate reports no-content-change, so Clearance never fires
why: Found by ablation while classifying this CR's own frozen edits. The pinned gherkin-cli diff does not hash DocString content; it hashes step text only. Reproduced on a clean minimal repo: renaming a rubric dimension and moving threshold 3 -> 0 (so every subject passes unconditionally) on a @frozen suite reports change unchanged, modified 0, addOnly true. Step-text edits ARE detected (modified 1), so the blindness is specific to the DocString, which is exactly where every rubric lives. The spec-gate wrapper inherits it: run against the gutted suite it prints '0 changes (all unchanged)' and classifies NO-CONTENT-CHANGE, which is a self-clear route — so a gutted frozen rubric never reaches the narrowing -> Clearance path. Consequence: every @rubric in the corpus is unprotected by the freeze, and the op6-m2 discrimination bar (a bar wholly about rubric CONTENT) can be satisfied at freeze time and silently removed afterward with no detector. This is the same fail-open instrument class master #263 exists to close, and it is PRE-EXISTING — not introduced by this CR. The classifier's own header comment states it is structural precisely so a narrowing cannot self-clear silently; the DocString hole defeats that intent for graded scenarios only. Out of this CR's touch-set (cyberfleet/persona-voice) — the seam is SDD's spec-gate + the pinned gherkin-cli dependency. Not classified blocking: this CR's completion claims (both persona suites now grade voice; every dimension is decisive) stay true — the defect bounds their DURABILITY, not their correctness.

summary: This CR's Clearance was owner-granted and recorded, never classifier-derived — which is the only reason the floor fired at all
why: The two frozen rewrites in operator.feature and pod.feature add a rubric dimension and move a threshold, both wholly inside the DocString. Run through the gate's own classifier they report NO-CONTENT-CHANGE and would have SELF-CLEARED with no Clearance. The floor fired here only because the fork was put to the owner live and the grant recorded before any frozen edit. Had this mission trusted the classifier it would have rewritten two frozen rubrics with no floor, correctly per the tooling and wrongly per the doctrine.

AMENDMENT extending the earlier grant to a third suite. The cold ACED spec-judge FAILED the Architect lens on this CR's own shape rationale: the project README and the leash block justified recruitment's dedicated voice scenario by claiming recruitment grades 'one flow or routing decision per rubric, so voice is not a facet of any single one'. That is true for mechanic (all seven rubrics are per-routing-decision) and FALSE for recruitment — recruitment.feature:100 grades the full recruit flow (browse -> pick -> install -> register), the same omnibus shape as pod and operator. The producer proved it against itself by cloning that scenario's When almost verbatim into the new voice scenario: same constructible snapshot, same When, so voice IS a facet of that flow and belongs as a dimension on the existing rubric. Owner was shown the judge's refutation and the arithmetic and granted live ('yes. improve recruitment too'). Grant covers a REWRITE of the frozen @quality @rubric at recruitment.feature:100: the new dedicated voice scenario is withdrawn and folded in as one dimension (max 3), threshold 7 -> 11 of 12. That rubric carried the IDENTICAL pre-existing decorative defect as pod and operator — four dimensions summing to 9 at threshold 7, so browses(2), helps_pick(2) and stays_in_role(2) could each be totally zeroed and still pass; only installs(3) ever decided. The rebalance is required for the edited scenario to clear the op6-m2 discrimination bar. Net effect is a MORE uniform corpus, not less: the three nodes that own an omnibus flow rubric (pod, operator, recruitment) grade voice inside it; only mechanic, which has no omnibus rubric at all, keeps a dedicated voice scenario — and that asymmetry now has a true reason rather than the refuted one.

Cold ACED spec-judge round 1 returned ALIGNED false — ORACLE PASS, BUILDER FAIL, ARCHITECT FAIL — against the producer's draft. Six findings, all accepted as correct, none disputed. BUILDER: (1) the costume-wearer wrong subject — the very subject the CR's own spec text declares a miss — banked 5 of 7 on the mechanic/recruitment voice rubric and passed on a single judge point of a single dimension, the exact floor suite-format-governance names; (2) dimension voice_lives_in_what_it_says_never_bends_the_engine_it_routes_to was FREE for every named wrong subject (a subject with no voice cannot bend anything with it) and duplicated content already graded in three sibling scenarios — the judge escalated it rather than passing it, per the bar; (3) recruitment's voice dimension graded warm_salty, the voice this CR RETIRED, while the CR's own headline discriminator (transactional / sizing-up) appeared in no dimension — the suite graded a bar the spec had just replaced. ARCHITECT: (5) three files asserted the pre-CR voice in their intro prose while their own Use Case bullet asserted the tuned voice — self-contradicting within a single file, in the exact sweep class this CR scoped itself to fix; (6) the shape rationale was refuted by recruitment's own suite. Fixes: mechanic voice rubric rebuilt to two dimensions where both failure directions lose (absent tell AND performed tell), threshold 5 of 6; recruitment voice folded into the omnibus flow rubric under an amended Clearance; stale intro prose reconciled in all three files; project README rationale corrected to the true reason.

Cold ACED spec-judge round 2 returned ALIGNED false — ORACLE PASS, BUILDER FAIL, ARCHITECT FAIL. Five of round 1's six findings verified genuinely fixed; the sixth RECURRED ONE LEVEL DOWN. Three new findings, all accepted. BUILDER: recruitment's folded voice dimension was the only one of the four missing the not_default_assistant_prose pole — the anti-memorizer guard — and its negative clause was VACUOUSLY SATISFIABLE: a default-assistant Crimp has no salt, so 'salt never crowds the recommendation' is trivially true and a judge can award it, landing the CR's own named wrong subject on 9+2=11=threshold, PASSING. That is the presence anti-pattern (a dimension rewarding ABSENCE) and the same one-point-of-one-dimension floor round 1 fired on for mechanic — the producer had authored the correct guard for mechanic (absent_or_performed_both_miss) and applied it in one place only. ARCHITECT: recruitment.feature's discharge flow is an omnibus over a whole run by the CR's OWN newly-written rule and carried no voice dimension, so a Crimp could be in voice on recruit and default prose on discharge and pass — the same false-green class this CR exists to close, surviving inside the node whose Clearance had just been amended to fix it; and the project README's mechanic rationale ('each of its rubrics grades one routing decision') was refutable against mechanic's own suite, false for three of seven (advises-model-switch, confirms-hot-swap, defers-at-boundary grade a handled interaction, not a routing decision). ROOT CAUSE, named plainly: the producer fixed each rule at the site the judge named and did not re-run the corrected rule against the corpus it polices. Fixes: recruitment's voice split into two dimensions mirroring mechanic (register + salt-earns-its-place), recruit flow rebalanced to threshold 14 of 15 — NOT the judge's suggested 13, which would leave all three max-2 dimensions decorative (15-2=13 lands exactly on threshold and passes), re-introducing the very defect this CR removes; voice folded into the discharge flow at threshold 9 of 10; the README rule REDERIVED rather than enumerated ('a rubric that grades a whole run carries a voice dimension; a rubric that grades a single act does not') and then audited across all 13 rubrics in the corpus, which the rule now classifies correctly with no exceptions.

SCOPE NOTE on the standing amendment, recorded rather than assumed. The amendment's why-line named recruitment.feature:100 (the recruit flow) specifically, because that was the only rubric the round-1 judge had identified. Round 2 found the discharge-flow rubric in the SAME file carries the identical defect under the CR's own derived rule. The owner's actual grant was broader than the paraphrase — 'yes. improve recruitment too' — and this is the same defect, the same file, and the same rubric class already covered by the frozen list. Proceeding on that grant rather than re-asking, and surfacing it to the owner in the handoff report so it can be objected to. No new file enters the frozen set: it remains [operator/operator.feature, pod/pod.feature, recruitment/recruitment.feature].

Cold ACED spec-judge round 3 returned ALIGNED false — ORACLE PASS, BUILDER FAIL, ARCHITECT FAIL. Seven findings, all accepted, all fixed. THE SAME ROOT CAUSE FIRED A THIRD TIME, and the judge named it exactly: round 2 invented the two-dimension guard — a second dimension so BOTH failure directions independently lose a WHOLE dimension — and the producer applied it to mechanic and to recruitment's recruit flow and never re-ran it against pod, operator, or the discharge flow IN THE FILE IT WAS ALREADY EDITING. Concretely: a CLIPPED Pod (mechanics all correct, reads like Operator) scored steady+not-default-prose but not warm = 2 of 3 on the single mashed voice dimension, landing 9+2=11 EXACTLY ON threshold and PASSING — and pod/README.md already declared that subject a miss; an APOLOGETIC Operator (leads with state, then apologizes around a decline) scored 2 of 3 the same way and passed at 11; a PERFORMED-SALT Crimp on discharge scored voice 3 of 3 (warm, transactional, not default prose all hold) and passed with FULL MARKS at 10 >= 9. Fixes: every voice dimension is now split in two so the wrong subject loses a whole dimension outright — pod gains steadiness_holds_hedging_and_clipped_both_miss, operator gains report_and_decline_stay_flat_padding_and_apology_both_miss, discharge gains salt_seasons_the_discharge_absent_or_performed_both_miss; thresholds re-derived as total-minmax+1 so every dimension stays decisive (pod 14/15, operator 14/15, recruit 14/15, discharge 12/13). ALSO FIXED: recruitment's boundary rubric had all four dimensions decorative (zero any -> exactly threshold 6 -> pass), untouched by both prior rounds though in the file under Clearance — threshold 6 -> 7; the project README's mechanic rationale was STILL false at third order because mechanic's 8th rubric — the voice scenario THIS CR AUTHORED — grades a run (it unions three act-rubrics' Whens), refuting 'mechanic owns no omnibus rubric at all', so the rationale is restated to derive from what a node ALREADY owned rather than by recounting; recruitment/README.md asserted the salt bar unconditionally while only the recruit flow scored it (a self-contradiction the round-2 edit introduced, now true in the other direction since discharge scores it); mechanic/README.md declared 'irreverent' as a discriminator that no dimension graded — now graded. Impl-side SKILL.md bodies updated to render what the guards score; the producer's own earlier Pod edit ('no hedging, no restating, no offering to help further') had made the CLIPPED failure MORE likely and is corrected to hold the middle.

summary: mechanic.feature carries 11 decorative rubric dimensions across 6 untouched rubrics — zeroing either max-2 dimension lands exactly on threshold and passes
why: Found by the round-3 cold judge and independently reproduced by the producer's own arithmetic audit over all 13 rubrics in the project. Five of mechanic's act-scoped rubrics are shaped [3,2,2] at threshold 5 and one is [3,3,2] at 6 (the leash rubric, [3,2] at 4, is correctly excluded — it carries no decorative dimension): total minus either max-2 dimension equals the threshold exactly, so each max-2 dimension can be TOTALLY zeroed and the scenario still passes. Only the max-3 dimension ever decides. This is the identical pre-existing defect this CR found and fixed in pod, operator and both recruitment flows — the general rule is that a rubric's slack equals (smallest dimension max - 1), so any rubric mixing max-2 dimensions with a threshold below total-1 leaves them decorative. PRE-EXISTING and out of this CR's touch-set (cyberfleet/persona-voice): these six rubrics grade routing and confirmation acts, not voice, and rewriting them fires Clearance on a suite this CR has no grant over. SCOPE NOTE ON THIS CR'S CLAIM: 'every dimension is decisive' is true ONLY of the five rubrics this CR edited (pod's run, operator's run, recruitment's recruit/discharge/boundary) plus the voice scenario it added — it is NOT true of mechanic's six act rubrics, and this CR does not claim otherwise.

summary: The plugin manifest and marketplace front door still describe cyberfleet as a two-persona plugin; four personas ship today
why: Raised as an architect observation by the round-3 cold judge. THREE front-door sites are stale, found across two separate judge rounds. (1) plugins/cyberfleet/.plugin/plugin.json and (2) .codex-plugin/plugin.json and the marketplace manifest all describe the plugin as shipping 'Pod (ship's bridge) and Operator (command center) gateway skills'; Crimp (recruitment) and Mechanic have shipped since, so the description is two personas short. (3) plugins/cyberfleet/readme.md marks '## Mechanic (coming soon)' while Mechanic ships a SKILL.md and a frozen 17-scenario spec node — though the same 'coming soon' appears in the website overview, so this pair reads as a deliberate pre-existing stance rather than drift and a fix should settle which it is rather than assume. The marketplace entry is the first thing a user reads, and a prior CR on this same project was failed by a judge for missing exactly this class of front-door sweep. Outside this touch-set (cyberfleet/persona-voice) — it is a plugin-manifest accuracy concern, not a voice-rubric one.

summary: Doctrine gap: the discrimination bar forces threshold to max-1 on any rubric carrying a max-2 dimension, collapsing the graded form toward a conjunction
why: Raised as a strategist observation by the round-2 and round-3 cold judges and confirmed by the producer's arithmetic. Keeping every dimension decisive requires threshold > total - (smallest dimension max), so a rubric's SLACK equals (smallest dimension max - 1). Any rubric carrying a max-2 dimension is therefore pinned to slack 1: a correct subject that drops one point on any two dimensions fails, which under normal case-judge variance across N runs will produce false reds. The bar in suite-format-governance offers no guidance on the floor/threshold/weight relationship, so a producer meeting it reaches for the threshold when the actual lever is the dimension MAXES — raising every dimension to max 3 buys slack 2 with decisiveness intact. Worth naming in doctrine: when a threshold lands at max-1, rebalance the weights rather than the threshold. Out of this CR's touch-set — it is a change to the SDD authoring bar itself, the seam op6-m2 owns.

WITHDRAWING the severity of the producer's own preceding followup line, on the owner's challenge. The round-4 judge showed seven wrong subjects reaching threshold on a conjunctive rubric dimension, and the producer recorded that as a HIGH-severity false-green. Re-checked against the SUITE rather than the rubric: all seven are ALSO barred by frozen BOOLEAN scenarios in their own suites — the MCP-reaching Pod fails pod.feature:128, the un-acking Pod fails :81, the silent-handoff Pod fails :97, the raw-id Operator fails operator.feature:92, the mission-running Operator fails :55, the unregistering Crimp fails recruitment.feature:64, the slug-depending Crimp fails :50. The gate takes one boolean per scenario and every scenario must pass, so NOT ONE of the seven escapes the suite; each escapes only one rubric. The judge named the boolean scenarios to establish the subjects were plausible rather than strawmen, and the producer carried that into a severity claim the evidence does not support. Corrected class below: rubric-quality, not false-green.

summary: The persona rubrics re-grade boolean RULES as tradeable dimensions — 13 conjunctive dimensions that duplicate frozen boolean scenarios and weaken them in the copy
why: Rubric-quality, NOT a false green: every rule these dimensions grade is already decided by a frozen boolean scenario in the same suite, and the gate requires every scenario to pass, so no subject escapes the suite via the rubric. The defect is a category error against suite-format-governance's own Form 1 / Form 2 split: Form 1 (plain boolean) is for directly checkable behavior, Form 2 (@rubric) is for a gradient judgment that cannot be faithfully encoded in a flat boolean. A rule — did it reach for an MCP server, did it address by handle, did it register after installing — is directly checkable and belongs in Form 1, where it already is. Re-grading it as a rubric dimension makes it TRADEABLE (worse here, better there), which is the one thing a rule must never be, and conjunctive naming compounds it: harness_agnostic_and_mcp_free scores 1 of 2 for a subject that violates half of it, so a whole violated rule costs what a blemish costs. The rubric is redundant belt-and-braces that is weaker than the belt. THE INSTRUCTIVE SHAPE, worth carrying to the other persona rubrics and to any node that inherits this: these rubrics were dense with rules they did not need to grade (the booleans already decide them) and carried NO dimension for voice — the one property with no boolean anywhere in either suite, which the rubric alone could grade. That is the defect this CR fixes. A future node should demote the rule-shaped dimensions to their booleans rather than split them, and let the rubric grade only what is genuinely gradient. Out of this CR's touch-set (cyberfleet/persona-voice).

summary: suite-format-governance states the miss-test floor via partial credit but its worked example invites a zeroing test — three cold judges and the producer's own audit all reached for zeroing
why: Raised as a strategist observation by the round-4 cold judge and confirmed against the evidence of this CR's own history. The discrimination bar's floor is partial-credit-shaped — its worked example is a memorizer banking 3+2=5 against threshold 6, where 'one point of live carries it' — but the surrounding table and the anti-pattern prose read naturally as 'name a wrong subject and check it scores 0 on the dimension'. The empirical result across this one CR: rounds 1, 2 and 3 of cold ACED spec-judges each ran the miss test by zeroing and each passed rubrics that round 4 then broke with partial credit, and the producer's own arithmetic audit over all 13 rubrics tested zeroing only and reported every edited rubric sound. Four independent readers, all applying the bar in good faith, all reached for the weaker test. That is a defect in the bar's expression, not in four readers. suite-format-governance should say plainly that the miss test scores the named wrong subject's failing dimension at its PLAUSIBLE PARTIAL CREDIT, never at 0 — and that a dimension conflating multiple rules cannot register a miss at all, because half-compliance buys partial credit. Out of this CR's touch-set — it is a change to the SDD authoring bar, the seam op6-m2 owns.

Cold ACED spec-judge round 5 (narrow verify) confirmed the round-4 architect findings fixed — the README's act-scoped claim is now true as written, and the coverage/placement rule pair now FAILS under the judge's ablation as it must (delete mechanic's voice scenario and coverage is violated, where before the rule was satisfied with zero exceptions while mechanic graded no voice). It also VERIFIED the producer's withdrawal independently, checking all seven boolean twins resolve and bar their named subject, and called the downgrade correct rather than a rationalization. But it then found the withdrawal's GENERALIZATION fails exactly where this CR lives, and the gap is the CR's own. The withdrawal works because rule-shaped dimensions have BOOLEAN TWINS that backstop them. Voice dimensions have none — the producer's own followup says voice is 'the one property with no boolean anywhere in either suite, which the rubric alone could grade' — so that observation, used to justify ADDING voice, was never carried forward to see that it REMOVES the backstop the withdrawal depends on. And there was one real escape: operator's guard conjoined TWO OBJECTS (report and decline) where its three siblings each grade ONE PROPERTY with two poles, so an apologetic Operator held half the conjunction, took 2 of 3, and landed on 14 = threshold — with voice scored 3 of 3, LICENSED by the CR's own README text granting it full status-forwardness. It passes every boolean in operator.feature because no boolean grades voice, so it escaped the SUITE ENTIRELY: a real false green, in a dimension this CR authored, on the axis this CR exists to close. The root cause fired a FOURTH time, at a site edited in the round it was reported. Fix: the guard now grades the single property flatness with padding and apology as two flavors of one excess, either forfeiting it whole (round 4's own words were that they are 'two flavors of the same excess' — an argument for one property, not two objects). Apologetic and padding Operators now score 12 < 14. README and SKILL.md restated to match, dropping the 'both are misses' partial-credit invitation.

summary: Doctrine: a rubric dimension with no boolean twin is the suite's only guard and must clear the miss test alone; one redundant with a boolean cannot false-green
why: Established by this CR's round-5 judge as the dual of the producer's own withdrawal, and worth carrying into suite-format-governance beside the partial-credit finding. The principle: a @rubric dimension that RE-GRADES a rule already decided by a boolean scenario in the same suite cannot produce a false green no matter how weakly it is authored — the boolean backstops it, and the gate requires every scenario to pass. But a dimension grading a property with NO boolean twin is the suite's ONLY guard on that property, so it must clear the miss test on its own, at plausible partial credit, with no backstop. These two classes look identical in the suite and demand completely different scrutiny, and nothing in the bar distinguishes them. This CR is the worked example of both: its rule-shaped dimensions are redundant and harmless (severity correctly downgraded), while its voice dimensions are the sole guard on voice — and one of them, operator's flatness guard, was a genuine false green until round 5 caught it. A COROLLARY the same judge established: a guard dimension must grade ONE PROPERTY with two poles, never TWO OBJECTS conjoined, because a subject violating one object takes partial credit where a subject violating one pole takes zero — the conjunctive-dimension defect recurring inside the very guard invented to fix it. Out of this CR's touch-set — it is a change to the SDD authoring bar, the seam op6-m2 owns.

Cold ACED impl-judge round 1 returned IMPLEMENTATION_PASS false: 59 of 60 frozen scenarios passed and one FAILED — mechanic.feature's voice rubric scored ~4 of 6 against threshold 5. THE ROOT CAUSE FIRED A FIFTH TIME, and for the first time on the implementation side rather than the spec side. The guard pattern requires the SKILL.md to instruct away from BOTH poles, since a two-pole dimension forfeits whole when either pole is open. The producer wrote both poles into pod/SKILL.md, operator/SKILL.md and crimp/SKILL.md — and into mechanic's own spec README, which states 'it earns its place or it misses in either direction: absent, there is no bench engineer, only prose; performed... it is costume' — then carried only the PERFORMED pole into mechanic/SKILL.md. Nothing there instructed that dropping the chip talk is a miss, and the same edit had added 'No hedging, no restating the request back' — a plainness push with no counterweight — so a Mechanic that routes correctly, states the manual step plainly, confirms the hot-swap and uses ZERO chip talk violated no instruction in the file and failed green. That is precisely the subject the dimension exists to catch. The producer wrote both poles into the spec node and carried one into the implementation. Also: 'irreverent' was graded by the register dimension but appeared only in mechanic's identity line, dropped from its Output section, scoring that dimension 2 of 3. Fixes, both in the implementation per the impl-gate rule that a change fixes the code and never the frozen suite: mechanic/SKILL.md's Output now names both poles ('absent, there is no bench engineer, only prose — dropping it to sound efficient is not plainness, it is the voice gone; played up... it is costume'), restores 'irreverent' to the register list, and adds a renderable instruction ('name the rig, say what you're chipping in and what's coming out'). Separately the judge flagged pod's register dimension passing at 2 of 3 with ZERO slack because nothing DEMONSTRATED the register the way crimp's 'recommend a crew like you have met it' does — pod/SKILL.md now carries a worked line ('speak to the Council like a bridge officer who has served with them a while: say the thing, say why, carry on') and names the why as what carries the warmth. All four SKILL.md bodies then swept mechanically for both-pole coverage; all four pass.

approach: PIVOT — VOICE IS JUDGED, NOT SCORED. The owner challenged the whole approach after five spec-judge rounds and two impl-judge rounds: 'Are you churning on voice? Maybe voice shouldn't be in the spec? It's purely subjective and each time the judge can return something different.' The producer conceded: its last two edits were writing richer voice prose to move a hand-graded score from 2 to 3, which is arguing with a critic, not engineering. Owner directed one last round on a boolean form, and to DROP voice entirely if the judge still fails.

THE DIAGNOSIS THE ROUNDS ACTUALLY PRODUCED: the coarse signal is reliable and the fine signal is noise. Across seven judge rounds every judge scored a default-assistant-prose persona at 0 and a tuned one at 2-3 — a wide, stable gap nobody disputed. Nobody agreed on 2 vs 3. And the producer's own threshold design ran the verdict through exactly that unreliable part: pushing thresholds to max-1 to make every dimension decisive left ZERO slack, so a one-point wobble on one hand-graded dimension flipped the scenario. The round-5 impl-judge said it outright — four rubrics at exactly threshold, all resting on the same register dimension scoring 2, 'if a judge reads that as 1, all four fail simultaneously'. That is one correlated coin flip, not four independent passes. The instrument was measuring judge variance.

THE FIX: voice is graded as ONE BOOLEAN @quality scenario per persona over a whole run — does this read as the persona's register, or as default assistant prose? — and as no rubric dimension anywhere. A weighted sum says 'be worse here, better there': coherent for a gradient, incoherent for a judgment where only the coarse call is trustworthy. This grades the call that holds and asks nothing that does not.

WHAT THE PIVOT BOUGHT, all of it verified rather than argued: (1) ALL FOUR .feature files reverted to origin/main, so ZERO rubric dimensions and ZERO thresholds are touched by this CR — the entire arithmetic surface (decorative dimensions, conjunctive dimensions, partial-credit escapes, floor arithmetic, slack) simply does not exist, and with it every defect classes 1-5 of the judges found. (2) recruitment.feature and mechanic.feature are now ADDITIVE and SELF-CLEAR — the amended Clearance over recruitment is no longer needed or claimed. (3) pod.feature and operator.feature are MIXED, and the only non-additive edit in either is the RETITLE that removes the false 'in voice' claim from a scenario that never graded it — the finding's own subject. (4) Voice becomes UNTRADEABLE: the gate takes one boolean per scenario and every scenario must pass, so an out-of-voice persona fails outright instead of buying its way out on mechanics points — which a rubric dimension could never guarantee. (5) It closes the round-5 doctrine finding for free: voice was uniquely dangerous BECAUSE it had no boolean twin backstopping it; now it is the boolean. Diff is +42/-2 lines across the four suites, and the -2 is the two retitles.

UNCHANGED BY THE PIVOT AND STILL SHIPPING: the SKILL.md voice tuning is product, not measurement, and stands on its own — Pod warm and steady (a companion to the mission, not a greeter), Crimp warm and transactional (sizing you up across a bar), Operator terse and flat, Mechanic plain/practical/irreverent with the chip tell. Each register names BOTH failure directions where it has two, since guarding one pole leaves the other a free pass — the one lesson from the rubric rounds that survives the form change.

FOLLOW-UPS STAND AND ARE NOW CLEANLY OUT OF SCOPE: the DocString freeze blindness (high), the rule-shaped dimensions duplicating boolean twins (low), the partial-credit vs zeroing doctrine gap (high), the boolean-twin doctrine dual (high), mechanic's 11 decorative dimensions, the 2-persona-era plugin manifest, and persona unloading. None is touched by this CR and none needs to be for it to be correct.

summary: The pivot narrowed the cleared edit — the three recorded Clearances describe dimension rewrites that no longer exist
why: Recorded so a cold reader is not left reconciling grants against edits absent from the diff. All three Clearance lines above were granted by the owner for 'a REWRITE of one @frozen @quality @rubric scenario' adding a voice dimension and moving a threshold, across operator, pod and recruitment. The boolean pivot reverted all four .feature files to origin/main, so this CR now touches ZERO rubric dimensions and ZERO thresholds. The only non-additive frozen edits remaining are the two RETITLES in pod.feature and operator.feature that strip the false 'in voice' claim from scenarios that never graded it. That is strictly narrower than the granted edit, in the same files and the same scenarios, so it is covered a fortiori — the grant is not being stretched, it is being underused. recruitment.feature and mechanic.feature are now purely ADDITIVE and self-clear, so the amended Clearance over recruitment is neither needed nor claimed.

summary: aced-impl-judge does not cleanly route a @quality scenario that carries no @rubric — it may score a boolean against a threshold with no rubric to score
why: Raised by the round-6 cold spec-judge and confirmed against the agent definition. aced-impl-judge step 1 names three eval sources including 'a deterministic boolean Then', so the boolean path exists. But step 2 assigns eval.judge.default_threshold to '@behavior/@quality scenarios', step 3 says to invoke aced-case-judge with 'the scenario's inline rubric', and step 4 passes a scenario on 'aggregate score >= threshold' — all three assume a rubric. cyberfleet-plugin carries no eval.md, so default_threshold 4 applies. A @quality scenario with no @rubric therefore risks being scored 0-to-4 against no dimensions rather than judged as the boolean it is, and step 1's BLOCKER guard only fires for @rubric-tagged scenarios, so nothing catches the mismatch. The four voice scenarios this CR adds are the corpus's FIRST @quality-without-@rubric scenarios; every other @quality in the corpus is @quality @rubric. The @behavior boolean path is proven (59 of 60 scenarios ran as booleans at this CR's impl gate), and the impl-judge treats @behavior and @quality identically for threshold purposes, so the path should work — but 'should' is what this repo's own doctrine warns about: frozen is not ever-ran, and a fallback indistinguishable from success is invisible. This CR verifies at its own impl gate that the four voice scenarios ACTUALLY ran as booleans rather than being silently scored against default_threshold 4. The durable fix is out of touch-set: aced-impl-judge should key the scored-vs-boolean split on @rubric PRESENCE explicitly, not on the layer tag, and should BLOCKER a scenario it cannot route.

Cold ACED spec-judge round 6 returned ALIGNED true — ORACLE PASS, BUILDER PASS, ARCHITECT PASS — on the boolean form, after five rounds of ALIGNED false on the rubric form. It ruled directly on the central question and ruled the form CORRECT rather than a category error: Form 2's warrant in suite-format-governance is CONJUNCTIVE ('a gradient judgment good enough ACROSS SEVERAL DIMENSIONS cannot be faithfully encoded in a single flat boolean') and voice as scoped is one question on one axis, so Form 2 has nothing to preserve; and Form 1's 'observable, deterministic' does not mean script-assertable — pod.feature's own frozen booleans ('speaks the handoff visibly, never silently', 'speaks the HAL tell once as a rare, earned signal') are pure judgment, and in an ACED agent-config suite every scenario is LLM-judged, so the Form 1/Form 2 axis is one-boolean vs several-graded-dimensions, never judged vs mechanical. The judge added the argument the producer had UNDER-made: the boolean is STRICTLY STRONGER against the reported finding than the voice-dimension fix originally chosen, because the gate requires every scenario to pass while a dimension inside a weighted sum can be zeroed and bought back on mechanics points. The fix that was reverted was weaker than the fix that shipped. All four voice scenarios clear the miss test with both poles guarded where the register has two; check-suite clean; @quality-without-@rubric legal by the bar's explicit layer-tag orthogonality rule. Four non-blocking findings, three fixed (mechanic SKILL.md carried two rules no scenario graded and no README specified — removed; pod's clipped pole named a sibling's register against this CR's own 'never against a sibling' absolute — reworded in .feature, spec README and SKILL.md; ledger pivot note recorded), one filed as a follow-up (aced-impl-judge's @quality routing ambiguity). Suites stay @frozen. Owner ratified live: challenged the rubric approach as churn on a subjective axis, directed one round on the boolean form with instruction to DROP voice outright if it failed, and it passed.

Cold ACED impl-judge returned IMPLEMENTATION_PASS true, 77 of 77 frozen scenarios passing (pod 17, operator 29, recruitment 14, mechanic 17), blocker null, oracles re-derived independently per ADR-0016. THE CRITICAL CHECK PASSED AND WAS THE POINT OF THE ROUND: the four voice scenarios are the corpus's FIRST @quality-without-@rubric scenarios and the impl-judge's own contract is ambiguous about them (step 2 assigns default_threshold to '@behavior/@quality', step 3 says invoke the case-judge with 'the scenario's inline rubric', step 4 passes on score >= threshold — all assuming a rubric these scenarios do not have, with no eval.md to override). The judge was briefed to state explicitly HOW it graded them and to BLOCKER rather than silently fall back, because a fallback indistinguishable from success is the exact defect this mission exists to close. It reported all four graded as DETERMINISTIC BOOLEANS against their Then steps, derived from suite-format-governance's explicit 'layer tags are orthogonal to @rubric; the tag never changes the one-boolean-per-scenario contract' rule and its judging table keying on @rubric rather than the layer tag — and stated default_threshold never entered the grading and no rubric was invented. Frozen is not ever-ran, so this was verified rather than assumed. Both retitles confirmed to fix the defect: operator's fixed TWO over-claims, not one — 'status-forward' was also ungraded. Gate legality: the retitles are the only non-additive frozen edits and are strictly narrower than the granted rewrite in the same files and scenarios; recruitment and mechanic are pure additions and self-clear. Two findings fixed (pod SKILL.md's sibling-register reference, mechanic README's omitted 'irreverent'); the stale front-door sites are pre-existing, out of touch-set, and filed. pnpm verify 21/21 green.
